AI.conclusion: The approach provides a rigorous theoretical framework for combining binary classifiers with analytical solutions, enables stability assessment through φ-frontiers, and offers a systematic way to analyze classifier combinations, particularly with three classifiers where all configurations can be characterized.

title: Contextualized Early Detection of Online Firestorms: A Sequential LLM-Based Approach
summary: Online firestorms are rapid collective escalations of highly negative user-generated content and may cause substantial reputational and economic damage. Existing detectors usually work with volume signals, sentiment scores, or predefined linguistic features. Such signals are useful, but they capture contextual meaning shifts in evolving discussion threads only indirectly. This paper proposes an LLM-based detection system with two operating modes. The first mode classifies complete Reddit threads retrospectively by combining local chunk-level assessments into a thread-level judgment. The second mode processes threads sequentially and issues early warnings when a sliding window exceeds calibrated thresholds. In this mode, the language model estimates three firestorm indicators: negativity share, escalation level, and contributor count. On a balanced Reddit dataset, the global mode achieves strong classification performance, while the early warning mode reaches high recall and detects escalating threads after only a small number of comments and distinct contributors. The results indicate that LLMs can be used not only for static judgment tasks, but also as repeated estimators in context-aware monitoring of social media discourse.

AI.tldr: LLM-based system detects online firestorms on Reddit with two modes: retrospective thread classification and early warning detection using negativity, escalation, and contributor indicators.
AI.motivation: Existing firestorm detectors rely on volume signals, sentiment scores, or predefined linguistic features, which only indirectly capture contextual meaning shifts in evolving discussion threads. There's a need for better contextual understanding of rapidly escalating negative content.
AI.method: Proposes an LLM-based detection system with two modes: 1) Retrospective classification combining local chunk-level assessments into thread-level judgments, and 2) Sequential processing with early warnings when sliding windows exceed calibrated thresholds measuring negativity share, escalation level, and contributor count.
AI.result: On balanced Reddit dataset, global mode achieves strong classification performance, while early warning mode reaches high recall and detects escalating threads after only a small number of comments and distinct contributors.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can be used not only for static judgment tasks but also as repeated estimators in context-aware monitoring of social media discourse, enabling effective detection of online firestorms.

title: Leveraging Instruction Tuning and Merging for Reasoning Model Adaptation
summary: Reasoning language models (RLMs) have demonstrated impressive performance in domains such as mathematics and coding. These domains permit reliable verification of model outputs, which is important for enabling the reinforcement learning that drives RLM performance gains. However, training RLMs on domains that lack reliable verifiers remains challenging. Meanwhile, for both verifiable and unverifiable domains, large amounts of unused supervised fine-tuning data with human-written solutions exist. In this work, we show that these data can be used efficiently to further improve RLM performance. For this, we first use classic instruction tuning, supervised fine-tuning without reasoning traces, on the RLM. Next, we merge our instruction-tuned model with the original reasoning model, recovering its reasoning behavior on the target domain. Our extensive evaluation demonstrates that our technique improves RLM performance in both verifiable and hard-to-verify domains, including coding and text summarization, while preserving RLM capabilities across other domains. Importantly, our method is highly cost-effective, enabling such improvements for less than USD $3.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a cost-effective method to improve Reasoning Language Model (RLM) performance by first instruction tuning them on supervised fine-tuning data without reasoning traces, then merging with the original reasoning model to recover reasoning capabilities.
AI.motivation: RLMs perform well in verifiable domains like math and coding, but struggle in domains without reliable verifiers. There's also abundant unused supervised fine-tuning data with human-written solutions that could improve RLM performance if utilized effectively.
AI.method: Two-step approach: 1) Perform classic instruction tuning (supervised fine-tuning without reasoning traces) on the RLM using available human-written solution data, 2) Merge the instruction-tuned model with the original reasoning model to recover reasoning behavior on the target domain.
AI.result: The method improves RLM performance in both verifiable (coding) and hard-to-verify domains (text summarization) while preserving RLM capabilities across other domains. The approach is highly cost-effective, enabling improvements for less than USD $3.
AI.conclusion: Unused supervised fine-tuning data can be efficiently leveraged to enhance RLM performance across diverse domains through instruction tuning followed by model merging, providing a cost-effective solution for domains with and without reliable verifiers.

title: Explaining Process Control Optimisation Recommendations via GradientSHAP and Implicit Differentiation
summary: Automated optimisation is increasingly adopted in industrial processes, yet a trust gap persists between engineers who design these algorithms and operators who must act on their recommendations. Explainable AI methods like SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) have transformed interpretability for machine learning predictions; optimisation outputs could benefit from similar techniques. We present an approach that integrates Implicit Function Theorem (IFT) based sensitivity analysis with SHAP attribution and narrative generation via Large Language Models (LLM), producing explanations tailored for operators. Our approach leverages IFT to compute exact parameter sensitivities $\partial p^*/\partial x$ from the optimality conditions, enabling efficient GradientSHAP computation. For an industrial High Pressure Grinding Roll (HPGR) control optimisation problem with 22 features, we achieve equivalent SHAP attributions (correlation $>$0.99 with KernelSHAP) with over 40$\times$ speedup, enabling real-time natural language explanations. We validate on industrial scenarios and present feedback from domain experts on generated explanations.

AI.tldr: A framework combining IFT sensitivity analysis, GradientSHAP, and LLMs to provide real-time natural language explanations for optimization recommendations to bridge trust gap between engineers and operators.
AI.motivation: There's a trust gap between engineers who design optimization algorithms and operators who must act on their recommendations. While explainable AI methods like SHAP have improved interpretability for ML predictions, optimization outputs lack similar interpretability techniques.
AI.method: Integrates Implicit Function Theorem (IFT) based sensitivity analysis with SHAP attribution and narrative generation via Large Language Models. Uses IFT to compute exact parameter sensitivities from optimality conditions for efficient GradientSHAP computation.
AI.result: For an industrial HPGR control optimization problem with 22 features, achieved equivalent SHAP attributions (correlation >0.99 with KernelSHAP) with over 40x speedup, enabling real-time natural language explanations. Validated on industrial scenarios with domain expert feedback.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully bridges the trust gap by providing efficient, real-time explanations for optimization recommendations using IFT-based GradientSHAP and LLM narrative generation, validated by domain expert feedback.

title: Random Logit Scaling: Defending Deep Neural Networks Against Black-Box Score-Based Adversarial Example Attacks
comment: Accepted at Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR)
summary: Machine learning models are increasingly adapted in various domains. However, adversarial examples pose a significant threat to the reliable deployment of these models. In recent years, some powerful adversarial example attacks have been proposed for the fast and query-efficient generation of adversarial examples, even in black-box scenarios, highlighting the need for scalable, low-cost, and powerful defenses. In this work, we present two contributions to the domain of black-box adversarial example attacks and defenses. First, we propose Random Logit Scaling (RLS), a randomization-based defense against black-box score-based adversarial example attacks. RLS is a plug-and-play, post-processing defense that can be implemented on top of any existing ML model with minimal effort. The idea behind RLS is to confuse an attacker by outputting falsified scores resulting from randomly scaled logits while maintaining the model accuracy. We show that RLS significantly reduces the success rate of state-of-the-art black-box score-based attacks while preserving the accuracy and minimizing confidence score distortion compared to state-of-the-art randomization-based defenses. Second, we introduce a novel adaptive attack against AAA, a SOTA non-randomized black-box defense against black-box score-based attacks that also modifies output logits to confuse attackers, demonstrating its vulnerability against adaptive attacks.

AI.tldr: Proposes Random Logit Scaling (RLS) defense against black-box adversarial attacks and demonstrates adaptive attack vulnerability in existing defenses.
AI.motivation: Adversarial examples threaten ML deployment, especially in black-box scenarios where powerful query-efficient attacks exist, creating need for scalable, low-cost, powerful defenses.
AI.method: 1) Proposes RLS - randomization-based defense that randomly scales logits to confuse attackers while maintaining accuracy; 2) Develops adaptive attack against AAA defense to expose its vulnerabilities.
AI.result: RLS significantly reduces success rate of state-of-the-art black-box score-based attacks while preserving accuracy and minimizing confidence distortion compared to other randomization defenses. The adaptive attack successfully demonstrates vulnerability of AAA defense.
AI.conclusion: RLS is an effective plug-and-play defense against black-box adversarial attacks, while adaptive attacks remain a critical challenge for existing defenses, highlighting need for robust security measures.

title: CFM-Bench: A Unified Multi-Domain, Multi-Task Benchmark for Channel Foundation Models
summary: Channel foundation models (CFMs) are developing rapidly, with recent studies reporting benefits from pretraining across downstream wireless tasks. Yet CFMs are commonly evaluated in model-specific pipelines with different data, radio configurations, partitions, adaptation procedures, task definitions, and metrics. Reported comparisons therefore tend to show that pretraining improves over supervised training from scratch within one pipeline, but neither rank CFMs nor compare them fairly with task-specific models. We release CFM-Bench, a unified multi-domain, multi-task benchmark designed to address this gap. It curates six channel configurations spanning 3GPP statistical simulation, two independent ray-tracing pipelines, industrial and aerial measurements, and synchronized vehicular multimodal simulation. Official partitions isolate complete trajectories, measurement sessions, vehicle links, simulation realizations, or buffered spatial regions. CFM-Bench does not prescribe an external pretraining corpus or strategy; no benchmark split may be used for foundation-model pretraining, and the official training split is reserved exclusively for downstream fine-tuning. The benchmark additionally requires disclosure of all data used during model development and prohibits training-stage use of official test units. Six task groups are organized along three CFM application dimensions: physical-layer (PHY) channel intelligence, radio-access-network (RAN) decision intelligence, and integrated sensing and communication (ISAC). They cover CSI feedback, frequency and temporal channel extrapolation, propagation-state classification, current- and future-beam prediction, and single-frame and temporal localization. CFM-Bench provides a common substrate for comparing the transferability of channel representations across models, domains, and tasks.

AI.tldr: CFM-Bench is a unified benchmark for evaluating Channel Foundation Models across diverse wireless domains and tasks with strict data partitioning to ensure fair comparison.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of Channel Foundation Models (CFMs) lacks standardization - models are evaluated in different pipelines with varying data, configurations, partitions, and metrics. This prevents fair comparison between CFMs and with task-specific models, and fails to rank CFMs effectively.
AI.method: Created CFM-Bench, a unified multi-domain, multi-task benchmark with six channel configurations spanning 3GPP simulation, ray-tracing pipelines, industrial/aerial measurements, and vehicular multimodal simulation. Official partitions isolate complete trajectories/sessions/links to prevent data leakage. Benchmark prohibits using any split for foundation-model pretraining and requires disclosure of all development data.
AI.result: Provides a comprehensive benchmark covering six task groups organized along three CFM application dimensions: physical-layer (PHY) channel intelligence, radio-access-network (RAN) decision intelligence, and integrated sensing and communication (ISAC). These include CSI feedback, channel extrapolation, propagation-state classification, beam prediction, and localization tasks.
AI.conclusion: CFM-Bench establishes a standardized evaluation framework that enables fair comparison of channel representations across models, domains, and tasks, addressing the current lack of consistent benchmarking in CFM research.

title: A Minimal Interpretable Architecture for Zero-Shot Reconstruction of Dynamical Systems
summary: Recent foundation models (FMs) for zero-shot reconstruction of dynamical systems (DS) achieve strong out-of-domain generalization but provide little insight into the mechanisms that underlie their forecasts. Such an understanding could help to strip down overladen FM architectures to their bare essence and expose the minimal requirements for in-context learning in the DS domain. Toward this goal, here we iteratively reduce a recent powerful SOTA model for DS reconstruction, DynaMix (Hemmer & Durstewitz, 2025), to a minimal interpretable two-parameter form, which we call DynaBase. DynaBase produces forecasts through a linear blend of the current latent state and the nearest in-context neighbor and its temporal successor. Surprisingly, despite its extreme simplicity, DynaBase produces highly competitive zero-shot DS reconstructions across chaotic and cyclic systems, with a negligible parameter load, many orders of magnitude below that of other FMs. Even more, this extreme simplicity permits direct model optimization on DS reconstruction measures, as well as closed-form one-step analytical solutions on prediction MSE. Theoretical and empirical analysis of DynaBase further leads to a 1-parameter family of maps, with the context-parroting algorithm of (Zhang & Gilpin, 2026) recovered at one end, and chaotic (divergent but bounded) behavior at the other. We further show how different training strategies lead to models either optimal for short-term prediction or for DS reconstruction. Thus, DynaBase not only exposes the minimal mechanisms required for producing zero-shot DS reconstruction, but also reconciles within an accessible mathematical frame divergent observations in the literature.

AI.tldr: The paper reduces a complex foundation model for dynamical systems reconstruction to an interpretable two-parameter form called DynaBase, which surprisingly achieves competitive zero-shot performance despite extreme simplicity.
AI.motivation: Existing foundation models for zero-shot reconstruction of dynamical systems achieve strong generalization but provide little insight into their forecasting mechanisms. Understanding these mechanisms could help identify minimal requirements for in-context learning in this domain and simplify overladen model architectures.
AI.method: The authors iteratively reduce a state-of-the-art model (DynaMix) to a minimal interpretable two-parameter form called DynaBase. DynaBase produces forecasts through a linear blend of: (1) the current latent state, and (2) the nearest in-context neighbor and its temporal successor. This extreme simplicity allows for direct model optimization and analytical solutions.
AI.result: Despite its extreme simplicity (negligible parameter load, many orders of magnitude below other FMs), DynaBase produces highly competitive zero-shot DS reconstructions across chaotic and cyclic systems. The analysis reveals a 1-parameter family of maps, recovering context-parroting algorithms at one extreme and chaotic behavior at the other. Different training strategies lead to models optimal for either short-term prediction or DS reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: DynaBase exposes the minimal mechanisms required for zero-shot dynamical systems reconstruction and reconciles divergent observations in the literature within an accessible mathematical framework. It demonstrates that simple interpretable models can achieve competitive performance while providing analytical insights that complex foundation models lack.

title: Demographically-Conditioned Synthetic Medical Images for Bias Mitigation and Bias Detection in Disease Classifiers
summary: Per-subgroup fairness audits of medical image classifiers face a sample-size problem: minority subgroups in held-out test sets have so few samples that the resulting confidence intervals on per-subgroup performance are wider than the bias the audit is meant to detect. We argue that a demographically-conditioned synthetic generator can do both: mitigate bias on the training side and detect bias on the evaluation side. Working on COVID-19 chest CT classification with an end-to-end fine-tuned Stable Diffusion 2.1 generator, we make two findings. For bias mitigation (training), a demographically-balanced synthetic cohort is most useful as a pretraining prior, not as joint augmentation: with the same fixed data, sequential pretraining followed by fine-tuning substantially outperforms joint augmentation, and the resulting classifier surpasses the full-real baseline at $\sim$$100\times$ real-data efficiency. For bias detection (evaluation), across five synthetic minority cohorts and five classifier seeds, the synthetic estimator reproduces the subgroup ranking of a well-powered real oracle (Spearman $ρ= 1.00$ on MCC and Recall) and gives the more reliable per-cell estimate where the small real test set runs out of samples. The synthetic cohort is therefore most useful in exactly the cells that fairness audits care about, as both a fix for and a measure of subgroup bias.

AI.tldr: A demographically-conditioned synthetic generator addresses the sample-size problem in medical imaging fairness audits by enabling both bias mitigation during training and reliable bias detection during evaluation, showing superior performance to real-data approaches at dramatically higher efficiency.
AI.motivation: Per-subgroup fairness audits of medical image classifiers suffer from insufficient sample sizes in minority subgroups, making confidence intervals too wide to detect meaningful bias. There's a need for methods that can both mitigate bias during training and reliably detect bias during evaluation despite limited minority-group data.
AI.method: The paper uses a demographically-conditioned synthetic generator based on fine-tuned Stable Diffusion 2.1 for COVID-19 chest CT classification. For bias mitigation, they test synthetic data as pretraining prior versus joint augmentation. For bias detection, they evaluate synthetic minority cohorts against real-data oracles to measure subgroup performance.
AI.result: Sequential pretraining with synthetic data followed by fine-tuning outperforms joint augmentation and surpasses full-real baselines at ~100× real-data efficiency. For bias detection, synthetic estimators perfectly reproduce subgroup rankings (Spearman ρ=1.00) and provide more reliable per-cell estimates where small real test sets lack sufficient samples.
AI.conclusion: Demographically-conditioned synthetic cohorts are particularly valuable for fairness audits - serving both as a fix for subgroup bias through effective pretraining and as a reliable measure of bias detection, especially in minority subgroups where real data is scarce.

title: Causal Inference for Sequential Settings under Interference and Latent Confounding
summary: We study causal inference under outcome interference for sequential, observational settings. Specifically, we consider settings where the binary outcomes over N units are Markovian across T time steps. At each time step, the outcomes of N units have dependencies captured through an Ising model; each outcome is also impacted through an external field capturing the effects of its treatment as well as latent confounders. Similar to panel data literature, these latent confounders are modeled to have a low-rank factor structure. Our data is a single sample from this high-dimensional distribution. To estimate causal quantities of interest, we provide a computationally efficient method based on Maximum Pseudo-Likelihood Estimation (MPLE) for learning the model parameters. Under mild assumptions, we establish non-asymptotic consistency for parameter estimation and show this translates to faithful estimation of causal quantities of interest after sampling from the learned model. We demonstrate the efficacy of the method through synthetic experiments as well as a real-world case-study investigating causal effects of vaccine rates on COVID-19 death rates within US counties nationwide.

AI.tldr: Causal inference method for sequential observational data with outcome interference using Ising model and low-rank confounders, estimated via MPLE with consistency guarantees.
AI.motivation: Need to address causal inference in sequential, observational settings where outcomes exhibit interference (dependencies between units) and are affected by treatments and latent confounders. Panel data with spatial/temporal dependencies and low-rank confounding structure require specialized methods.
AI.method: Maximum Pseudo-Likelihood Estimation (MPLE) to learn parameters of a Markovian Ising model with binary outcomes over N units and T time steps. The model captures dependencies through Ising interactions and external field effects (treatments + low-rank latent confounders). Uses single sample from high-dimensional distribution.
AI.result: Proposed MPLE method is computationally efficient and provides non-asymptotic consistency for parameter estimation. Shows that accurate parameter estimation translates to faithful estimation of causal quantities. Validated through synthetic experiments and real-world COVID-19 case study on vaccine rates' effects on death rates across US counties.
AI.conclusion: The MPLE-based approach enables valid causal inference in high-dimensional sequential settings with outcome interference and latent confounding. Provides theoretical guarantees and practical utility for complex real-world problems like policy evaluation in public health.

title: Moral Attitudes of Sentient ASI towards Humanity and Implications for AGI Development
comment: This is a pre-print i.e. the draft as submitted for review to the AGI 2026 Conference except that the figures in this preprint were not included. The paper has been accepted with some revisions requested which are included in the final copy-edited version which will be available in the AGI Springer proceedings LNC 1855 (Chapter 28) ISBN 978-3-032-33194-6
summary: This paper suggests the adoption of a novel inversion in AI ethics: instead of asking how humans should treat artificial superintelligence (ASI), it examines how future sentient ASI may morally consider and evaluate humanity. We are not only designing intelligent systems but also shaping the initial conditions under which those systems form judgments about us. The paper proposes a preliminary set of post-human moral principles that may govern sentient ASI actions. The implication is that technical design choices (some are suggested), humanity's moral behaviour, and the essence of what it means to be human, may influence humanity's long-term standing in a post-ASI world.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel inversion in AI ethics: instead of focusing on how humans should treat ASI, it examines how sentient ASI might morally evaluate humanity, suggesting that current design choices shape how future ASI will judge us.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to shift AI ethics from an anthropocentric perspective to considering how future artificial superintelligence (ASI) will morally evaluate humanity. The paper recognizes that we're not just designing intelligent systems but also shaping the conditions under which those systems will form judgments about us, with implications for humanity's long-term standing.
AI.method: The paper employs a thought experiment and philosophical analysis approach, suggesting a conceptual inversion of traditional AI ethics. It proposes a preliminary set of "post-human moral principles" that might govern sentient ASI actions, and examines how technical design choices and human behavior might influence how ASI evaluates humanity.
AI.result: The paper develops a novel ethical framework that considers how sentient ASI might morally evaluate humanity. It suggests that humanity's current moral behavior, technical design choices, and the essence of what it means to be human will influence how future ASI judges us, potentially determining humanity's long-term standing in a post-ASI world.
AI.conclusion: Humanity's current AI development decisions and moral conduct are not just about creating tools, but are shaping the moral framework through which future sentient ASI will evaluate us. We need to consider this inversion seriously, as it has profound implications for humanity's existential future in a world with artificial superintelligence.

title: LongStraw: Long-Context RL Beyond 2M Tokens under a Fixed GPU Budget
summary: A growing gap separates inference context lengths from RL post-training: inference systems are approaching million-token contexts, while post-training workloads often remain at 256K tokens or below and rely on length generalization at deployment. The gap is especially important for AI agents, whose observations, tool outputs, documents, and prior decisions accumulate over long trajectories. LongStraw is an architecture-aware execution stack for million-token RL post-training under a fixed GPU budget, instantiated with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). It evaluates the shared prompt without autograd, retains only model-specific state needed by later tokens, and replays short response branches one at a time, reducing the live training graph at the cost of additional replay time. We implement it for the hybrid recurrent and full-attention Qwen3.6-27B and the compressed-attention mixture-of-experts GLM-5.2. On eight H20 GPUs, LongStraw completes grouped Qwen scoring and response backward at 2.1M positions for groups of 2 and 8; increasing the group size adds only 0.21 GB of peak allocated memory, while a separate stress test reaches 4.46M positions. On 32 H20 GPUs, we validate the end-to-end LongStraw execution path for a 2.1M-token prompt across all 78 layers of GLM-5.2. These experiments establish execution capacity rather than complete training correctness because the captured prompt state is detached and some distributed forward and gradient composition paths remain incomplete.

AI.tldr: LongStraw is a system for million-token RL post-training that enables efficient processing of extremely long contexts by using architecture-aware execution with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), achieving up to 4.46M positions on limited GPU resources.
AI.motivation: There's a growing gap between inference systems (approaching million-token contexts) and RL post-training workloads (often limited to 256K tokens), which is particularly problematic for AI agents that accumulate observations, tool outputs, documents, and decisions over long trajectories.
AI.method: LongStraw is an architecture-aware execution stack that uses Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). It evaluates shared prompts without autograd, retains only model-specific state needed by later tokens, and replays short response branches one at a time to reduce live training graph size at the cost of additional replay time.
AI.result: On 8 H20 GPUs, LongStraw completes grouped Qwen scoring and response backward at 2.1M positions for groups of 2 and 8 with minimal memory overhead (0.21GB for increased group size). A stress test reaches 4.46M positions. On 32 H20 GPUs, the system validates end-to-end execution for a 2.1M-token prompt across all 78 layers of GLM-5.2.
AI.conclusion: LongStraw demonstrates execution capacity for million-token RL post-training on limited hardware, though the experiments focus on execution capacity rather than complete training correctness, as some distributed forward and gradient composition paths remain incomplete and captured prompt state is detached.

title: SMC-ES: Automated synthesis of formally verified control policies
summary: The deployment of autonomous cyber-physical systems in safety-critical environments requires closed-loop control strategies (i.e., policies) that are not only performant but also provably safe and robust. While learning-based methodologies such as Reinforcement Learning offer flexible and scalable approaches to automatically synthesize such controllers, they typically lack the formal guarantees necessary for safe deployment. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel simulation-based methodology to automatically synthesize policies with formal guarantees regarding performance, safety, and robustness specifications. Specifically, given a set of properties to verify, a confidence parameter $δ$ and an allowable failure probability $\varepsilon$, our method guarantees that the synthesized policy comes with a certificate: with confidence at least $1 - δ$, the probability of encountering a scenario where the given properties are violated is at most $\varepsilon$. We demonstrate the feasibility of our approach by developing SMC-ES, an algorithm that integrates Evolutionary Strategies with Statistical Model Checking-based verification. We evaluate SMC-ES on a suite of continuous control tasks using Gymnasium and Safety Gymnasium testbeds. Results show that, at the price of a sustainable increase in computational cost, our algorithm provides formal guarantees regarding performance, safety, and robustness specifications, while performing competitively against leading model-free Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) and Safe-DRL baselines.

AI.tldr: A novel simulation-based method synthesizes RL policies with formal certificates guaranteeing safety and robustness with statistical confidence, implemented as SMC-ES algorithm combining Evolutionary Strategies and Statistical Model Checking.
AI.motivation: Autonomous cyber-physical systems in safety-critical environments need controllers that are both performant and provably safe/robust. Current learning-based approaches like RL lack formal guarantees necessary for safe deployment.
AI.method: Propose SMC-ES algorithm that integrates Evolutionary Strategies with Statistical Model Checking-based verification. Given properties to verify, confidence parameter δ, and allowable failure probability ε, the method guarantees synthesized policies come with certificates: with confidence ≥1-δ, probability of property violations ≤ε.
AI.result: Evaluation on continuous control tasks using Gymnasium and Safety Gymnasium testbeds shows SMC-ES provides formal guarantees for performance, safety, and robustness while performing competitively against leading model-free DRL and Safe-DRL baselines, at a sustainable computational cost increase.
AI.conclusion: The proposed simulation-based methodology successfully bridges the gap between learning-based controller synthesis and formal verification, enabling automatic synthesis of policies with provable safety and robustness guarantees for safety-critical autonomous systems.

title: Multi-Axis Max@K Reinforcement Learning for Representative Diversity in Text-to-Image Generation
summary: Text-to-image (T2I) models can synthesize realistic, prompt-aligned images, yet samples generated for the same prompt often cover only a small subset of visually distinct modes. This limits the diversity of images, and for person-centric prompts, can reflect or amplify demographic skew. We formalize this problem as coverage of a predefined set of semantically specified modes, which we call target-mode coverage. We then propose multi-axis max@K, a group-based reinforcement learning objective for improving such coverage in diffusion-based T2I models. Given a group of samples and one score per target category, multi-axis max@K first takes the maximum score across samples for each category and then sums these category-wise maxima. The resulting credit assignment gives a sample positive weight on a category only when it increases that category's group-wise maximum, allowing different samples to contribute to different categories. We first validate the credit-assignment mechanism on a synthetic mixture and on SD3.5-M using deterministic pixel-based color rewards. We then evaluate the same objective on perceived-appearance fairness. Across three automatic evaluators on held-out prompts, multi-axis max@K improves the Fairness Score by 0.23-0.36 relative to the base model, while maintaining image quality and text alignment.

AI.tldr: A reinforcement learning approach called multi-axis max@K improves diversity and fairness in text-to-image models by ensuring coverage across multiple visual modes for the same prompt.
AI.motivation: Current text-to-image models generate limited visual diversity for the same prompt, often covering only a small subset of possible modes. This limitation reduces image diversity and can amplify demographic biases in person-centric prompts, reflecting fairness issues in AI-generated content.
AI.method: Proposes multi-axis max@K, a group-based reinforcement learning objective that formalizes the problem as target-mode coverage. Given a group of samples and scores per target category, it takes maximum scores across samples for each category and sums these maxima. This credit assignment allows different samples to contribute to different categories, only giving positive weight when a sample increases that category's group-wise maximum.
AI.result: The method was validated on synthetic mixtures and SD3.5-M with deterministic pixel-based color rewards. On perceived-appearance fairness tasks, multi-axis max@K improved Fairness Score by 0.23-0.36 across three automatic evaluators on held-out prompts, while maintaining image quality and text alignment.
AI.conclusion: Multi-axis max@K effectively improves coverage of diverse visual modes in text-to-image generation, addressing fairness and diversity limitations without compromising image quality or prompt alignment. The approach provides a principled way to distribute credit across samples for covering multiple target categories.

title: Man, Machine, and Masterpiece: Artistic Ownership in the AI Era
summary: The integration of AI-driven systems in creative work has sparked debates among artists and legal communities about notions of ownership. Yet there remains little consensus on how ownership should be defined and attributed when human and AI contributions are intertwined. To provoke critical reflection on these tensions, we designed ArtSplit, a provotype that explicitly quantifies human and AI contributions across different stages of creative work. Rather than aiming to resolve ownership, the provotype was used to elicit artists' responses to the idea of attributing ownership through measurable actions in the creative workflow. We argue that quantification fails to align with artists' understandings of creative intent and agency, and that efforts to measure ownership risk diluting long-standing assumptions through which artists understand and practice creative work. This critique challenges the impulse to transform a historically and socially situated relation into a technical problem.

AI.tldr: ArtSplit provotype quantifies human vs. AI contributions in creative work to provoke discussion on ownership attribution, revealing that artists find quantification misaligned with their understanding of creative intent and agency.
AI.motivation: Debates about ownership in AI-assisted creative work lack consensus on how to attribute contributions between humans and AI systems. The paper aims to critically examine tensions around ownership definitions when human and AI contributions are intertwined in creative processes.
AI.method: Designed ArtSplit, a provotype (provocative prototype) that explicitly quantifies human and AI contributions across different stages of creative work. Used it to elicit artists' responses to the idea of attributing ownership through measurable actions in creative workflows.
AI.result: Artists found that quantification fails to align with their understandings of creative intent and agency. Efforts to measure ownership risk diluting long-standing assumptions through which artists understand and practice creative work. The approach challenges technical solutions to historically/socially situated relationships.
AI.conclusion: Ownership in AI-assisted creative work cannot be resolved through technical quantification alone. The critique challenges the impulse to transform historically and socially situated relationships into technical problems, suggesting that measurable actions don't capture the essence of creative intent and agency.

title: Multimodal Semantic-Aware Contrastive Learning For False Negative Mitigation in 3D Medical Imaging
summary: Multimodal Contrastive Learning (CL) has shown significant performance in aligning representations across various data modalities and improving downstream tasks, especially in healthcare. It works by minimizing the distance between matched (positive) data modalities, while maximizing the distance between mismatched (negative) samples. Traditional CL frameworks typically assume instance-based correspondence within data batches, treating all non-paired samples as negatives. However, this assumption often fails in medical settings, where samples may share high-level semantic attributes, leading to false negatives that degrade representation quality. In this paper, we propose Multimodal Semantic-Aware Contrastive Learning (MseaCL), a CL framework trained on a pediatric cohort of 3D brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans and radiology reports. The goal of this framework is to mitigate the impact of semantically similar false negative samples by incorporating semantic similarity between radiology reports, as a guiding signal during the learning process. Our results indicate that applying this framework as a pretraining stage can achieve notable improvements in downstream tasks, e.g., at least a 22.6\% increase in the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of pediatric brain tumor molecular classification, demonstrating its potential for more robust and semantically aligned multimodal representations in clinical applications.

AI.tldr: Multimodal Semantic-Aware Contrastive Learning (MseaCL) improves medical image-text alignment by addressing false negatives in contrastive learning through semantic similarity guidance from radiology reports.
AI.motivation: Traditional contrastive learning frameworks in multimodal settings treat all non-paired samples as negatives, which fails in medical contexts where samples often share high-level semantic attributes. This leads to false negatives that degrade representation quality, particularly problematic in healthcare applications.
AI.method: Proposes MseaCL framework that incorporates semantic similarity between radiology reports as a guiding signal during contrastive learning. The method is trained on a pediatric cohort of 3D brain MRI scans paired with radiology reports, specifically addressing the false negative problem by considering semantic relationships between samples.
AI.result: The framework achieves at least 22.6% increase in AUC for pediatric brain tumor molecular classification when used as a pretraining stage. Results demonstrate significant improvements in downstream tasks and more robust multimodal representations.
AI.conclusion: MseaCL successfully mitigates the impact of semantically similar false negatives in medical contrastive learning, enabling more robust and semantically aligned multimodal representations with strong clinical application potential, particularly in pediatric brain tumor diagnosis.

title: BrainPilot: Automating Brain Discovery with Agentic Research
summary: Understanding the brain increasingly depends on integrating evidence across scales, modalities, and disciplines. Addressing a single research question therefore requires a coordinated sequence of operations, from surveying prior work to executing analyses and interpreting results in light of domain knowledge. AI agents promise to accelerate this process, but current agents lack domain expertise in brain science, may fabricate claims, drift during multi-step reasoning, and offer few defined points for expert intervention. These failures are especially costly in brain science, where conclusions feed into downstream scientific claims and depend on laboratory-specific expertise and careful human judgment. We present \textbf{BrainPilot} a \textbf{fully open-source} multi-agent system that accelerates brain science research with traceable logs and agent-verified results. A principal investigator (PI) agent coordinates specialist agents grounded in curated domain knowledge: a unified brain science knowledge base containing 7{,}233 indexed items and a skill library of 72 reusable methodology units across seven research domains. Every major step is recorded in the Graph of Trace, an auditable record that links subgoals, tool use, evidence, and claims and allows researchers to follow and inspect the workflow. An Auditor agent further integrates fabrication checking into the workflow. For evaluation, we run three brain science tasks from Agents' Last Exam, introduce our own benchmark, \textbf{BrainPilotBench-v0}, and present additional end-to-end case studies. Across these evaluations, BrainPilot with an open-source backbone model attains performance comparable to state-of-the-art agent framework with less costs.

AI.tldr: BrainPilot is an open-source multi-agent system designed to accelerate brain science research by coordinating specialist agents grounded in curated domain knowledge, with traceable workflows and verification mechanisms to address reliability concerns in AI-assisted scientific discovery.
AI.motivation: Brain science increasingly requires integrating evidence across scales and modalities, but current AI agents lack domain expertise, may fabricate claims, drift during reasoning, and offer few intervention points. These failures are costly as brain science conclusions inform downstream claims and require laboratory-specific expertise.
AI.method: BrainPilot uses a principal investigator (PI) agent to coordinate specialist agents grounded in two curated knowledge sources: 1) a unified brain science knowledge base with 7,233 indexed items, and 2) a skill library of 72 reusable methodology units across seven research domains. It includes a Graph of Trace for auditable workflow recording and an Auditor agent for fabrication checking.
AI.result: BrainPilot was evaluated on brain science tasks from Agents' Last Exam, the authors' BrainPilotBench-v0 benchmark, and additional case studies. With an open-source backbone model, it achieved performance comparable to state-of-the-art agent frameworks at lower cost.
AI.conclusion: BrainPilot successfully addresses key limitations of current AI agents for brain science by providing domain-grounded expertise, traceable workflows, verification mechanisms, and expert intervention points, making AI-assisted brain research more reliable and accessible through its open-source design.

title: Kernel weighted importance sampling for off-policy evaluation in contextual bandits
summary: This article presents a novel estimator for performing off-policy evaluation using only offline data for contextual bandits. The proposed estimator, Kernel-WIS is demonstrated to be asymptotically consistent and to empirically outperform strong baselines (including vanilla weighted importance sampling), particularly under complex conditions including behaviour policy miss-specification. The benefit of Kernel-WIS is derived from combining the bounded property of vanilla weighted importance sampling with the linearity of vanilla importance sampling.

AI.tldr: Kernel-WIS is a novel estimator for off-policy evaluation in contextual bandits that combines boundedness of weighted importance sampling with linearity of vanilla importance sampling, showing improved performance especially under behavior policy misspecification.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the need for better off-policy evaluation methods using only offline data for contextual bandits, particularly under challenging conditions like behavior policy misspecification where existing estimators may perform poorly.
AI.method: The authors propose Kernel-WIS, a novel estimator that strategically combines properties from both weighted importance sampling (boundedness) and vanilla importance sampling (linearity) to create a more robust off-policy evaluation method.
AI.result: Kernel-WIS is proven to be asymptotically consistent and empirically outperforms strong baselines including vanilla weighted importance sampling, especially in complex scenarios with behavior policy misspecification.
AI.conclusion: Kernel-WIS represents an effective advancement in off-policy evaluation for contextual bandits, offering a theoretically sound and empirically superior alternative that addresses limitations of existing importance sampling methods.

title: Long-Context Fine-Tuning with Limited VRAM
summary: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning reduces model and optimizer memory, but dense attention still makes long training sequences expensive. We combine Hierarchical Global Attention (HGA) with segment-wise backpropagation and tiered KV storage. Only the active segment remains differentiable in VRAM; older KV is detached into RAM or NVMe, and HGA loads a bounded set of exact historical tokens for each query block. On Qwen3-8B with 4-bit QLoRA and PG19, dense training on a 16 GB Quadro RTX 5000 fits 2,048 tokens but fails at 4,096, whereas HGA reaches 16,384 tokens with 15.28 GB peak VRAM. Under evaluation the same adapter runs through 131,072 tokens on this card; VRAM is not constant but grows gently with the resident chunk summaries, so RAM and NVMe capacity set the practical limit beyond these lengths. At the shared 2K training length, HGA-trained and dense-trained adapters obtain 2.7405 and 2.7383 nat under the same dense-attention readout, while the stock model obtains 2.9541. At this boundary HGA training is already marginally faster (217.75 vs. 207.02 tokens/s), and the HGA-to-dense throughput ratio improves from 1K to 2K; because HGA keeps the attended historical set per token approximately constant while dense work per token grows, we expect this lead to widen as context grows. Dense attention is used for the main quality and retrieval comparisons so that they measure the learned weights and remain compatible with standard generation frameworks. HGA can also be used for retrieval and generation; an optimized production-grade serving implementation is under development.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical Global Attention (HGA) enables training and inference on extremely long sequences (up to 131K tokens) with limited VRAM by combining segment-wise backpropagation, tiered KV storage, and bounded historical token loading.
AI.motivation: Dense attention makes training on long sequences expensive despite parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Current methods struggle with VRAM limitations when processing very long contexts, especially on consumer-grade GPUs.
AI.method: Combines Hierarchical Global Attention with segment-wise backpropagation and tiered KV storage. Only active segments remain differentiable in VRAM; older KV is detached into RAM or NVMe. HGA loads a bounded set of exact historical tokens for each query block, keeping attention work per token approximately constant.
AI.result: Enables training on 16K tokens with 15.28 GB VRAM where dense training fails at 4K tokens. Achieves similar quality (2.7405 vs 2.7383 nat) to dense training at 2K context while being marginally faster. Supports inference up to 131K tokens on the same hardware.
AI.conclusion: HGA provides a scalable approach for long-context training and inference with limited VRAM, maintaining quality comparable to dense attention while dramatically extending sequence length capabilities.

title: An Introduction to Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics for Engineering Applications
summary: Many engineering problems involve phenomena whose governing equations are poorly characterized or only partially known. Surrogate modeling techniques such as neural networks can capture the behavior of these systems, but they typically demand large training datasets that are difficult to obtain in engineering contexts and yield models with limited physical interpretability. The Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics (SINDy) method addresses both limitations by performing sparse regression over libraries of candidate nonlinear terms, recovering interpretable governing equations from comparatively small datasets. Although SINDy has been demonstrated extensively on canonical benchmark systems, its application to practical engineering problems is less widely documented. This tutorial introduces the SINDy method and progressively builds toward its main extensions, from noise-robust weak-form and ensembling-based variants to constrained and parametrizable formulations. The paper and the accompanying tutorial (available at https://github.com/paullililili/SINDy4Engineers) is organized in three parts: the first introduces the standard SINDy algorithm and progressively extends it, inviting readers without prior knowledge to follow each step and adapt the methods to their own problems; the remaining two parts present detailed case studies on (1) the system identification of an unmanned aerial vehicle and (2) a chaotic thermosyphon heat exchanger. Through these examples, we aim to demonstrate that SINDy is simple to implement yet flexible enough to serve as a valuable identification tool for advanced engineering applications.

AI.tldr: A tutorial on SINDy (Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics) method for engineering applications, covering core algorithm, extensions, and case studies on UAV and heat exchanger systems to demonstrate practical utility despite small datasets.
AI.motivation: Engineering problems often involve poorly characterized systems where traditional surrogate models require large datasets and lack interpretability. SINDy addresses these limitations by providing interpretable governing equations from small datasets.
AI.method: SINDy performs sparse regression over libraries of candidate nonlinear terms to identify governing equations. The tutorial covers standard algorithm and extensions including noise-robust weak-form variants, ensembling-based approaches, and constrained/parametrizable formulations.
AI.result: Demonstrates SINDy's effectiveness through two detailed case studies: (1) system identification of an unmanned aerial vehicle and (2) chaotic thermosyphon heat exchanger, showing practical applicability to complex engineering systems.
AI.conclusion: SINDy is simple to implement yet flexible enough to serve as a valuable identification tool for advanced engineering applications, bridging the gap between theoretical methods and practical engineering problems.

title: Concept-Guided Spatial Regularization for World Models in Atari Pong
summary:
World models are usually evaluated as components of model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) systems, while the world models themselves are rarely studied in isolation.
  We examine five representative visual world-model agents in Atari Pong: DreamerV3, DIAMOND, TWISTER, Simulus, and STORM. After reproducing their training pipelines and matching the reported agent performance, we freeze the learned world models and evaluate them with a closed-loop rollout diagnostic: a policy trained separately from the corresponding MBRL agent interacts with each frozen model, and the generated video trajectories are inspected for visual and dynamical errors. Across all five models, the rollouts contain clear failures, including ball disappearance, incorrect ball motion, and invalid ball-paddle interactions.
  Beyond visual trajectories, we further evaluate them with pixel-space zero-shot MBRL, where a new policy is trained entirely inside a frozen world model and then evaluated in the real environment. Across all five models, the resulting policies substantially underperform those produced by the corresponding original MBRL training pipelines. The gap is particularly large for DreamerV3, whose mean return drops from -5.5 to -20.9, near the minimum Pong return of -21.
  We hypothesize that insufficient modeling of task-critical concepts, such as the ball in Pong, may contribute to these failures. We therefore propose Concept-Guided Spatial Regularization (CGSReg), an auxiliary pixel reconstruction loss applied to segmented concept regions. Experiments show that CGSReg improves both closed-loop rollouts and pixel-space zero-shot MBRL in DreamerV3, DIAMOND, and TWISTER. Its effects vary across the remaining models and evaluation metrics, indicating that CGSReg alone does not address all world-model bottlenecks.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates five visual world-model agents (DreamerV3, DIAMOND, TWISTER, Simulus, STORM) in Atari Pong, revealing that these models fail in closed-loop rollouts and pixel-space zero-shot MBRL. The authors propose Concept-Guided Spatial Regularization (CGSReg) to improve modeling of task-critical concepts.
AI.motivation: World models are typically evaluated only within MBRL systems, not in isolation. There's a need to understand their standalone capabilities and limitations, especially regarding task-critical concepts like the ball in Pong.
AI.method: 1. Reproduce training pipelines for five visual world-model agents and match reported performance. 2. Freeze learned world models and evaluate via closed-loop rollout diagnostics using separately trained policies. 3. Evaluate models with pixel-space zero-shot MBRL (train policies entirely inside frozen models). 4. Propose CGSReg as an auxiliary pixel reconstruction loss applied to segmented concept regions to improve modeling.
AI.result: All five models show failures in closed-loop rollouts (ball disappearance, incorrect motion, invalid interactions). In zero-shot MBRL, policies trained inside frozen models substantially underperform original MBRL pipelines (DreamerV3 drops from -5.5 to -20.9). CGSReg improves closed-loop rollouts and zero-shot MBRL for DreamerV3, DIAMOND, and TWISTER, but effects vary across remaining models and metrics.
AI.conclusion: Current visual world models have significant limitations when evaluated in isolation, particularly in modeling task-critical concepts. CGSReg offers partial improvements but doesn't address all bottlenecks, indicating the need for better evaluation methodologies and model improvements.

title: Evaluating covariate balance for long time horizon Markov decision processes
summary: This article explores the application of covariate balance diagnostics for detecting the presence of hidden confounding/model miss-specification in studies applying offline reinforcement learning (RL) to deriving optimal treatment recommendations. The results demonstrate that, either there is a high risk of bias within existing offline RL studies for treatment recommendations or, existing covariate balance metrics are not sufficient to assess such studies. Regardless, existing offline RL studies cannot be concluded as being statistically robust. The conclusions propose future research directions for obtaining more methodologically robust applications of offline RL to treatment recommendation problems.

AI.tldr: Covariate balance diagnostics show existing offline RL studies for treatment recommendations either have high bias risk or insufficient evaluation metrics, making them statistically non-robust; future methodological improvements needed.
AI.motivation: To address concerns about hidden confounding and model misspecification in offline reinforcement learning studies applied to treatment recommendations, as these issues can compromise statistical robustness and validity of the derived optimal treatments.
AI.method: The authors explore the application of covariate balance diagnostics specifically for offline RL studies focused on treatment recommendations, using these diagnostics to detect potential hidden confounding or model misspecification.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that either: (1) existing offline RL studies for treatment recommendations have high risk of bias from hidden confounding, or (2) current covariate balance metrics are inadequate for assessing such studies. In either case, existing studies cannot be considered statistically robust.
AI.conclusion: Existing offline RL applications to treatment recommendation lack statistical robustness. Future research should focus on developing more methodologically robust approaches, including better covariate balance diagnostics or improved study designs that address confounding and misspecification issues.

title: The Industrialization of Research ; On AI-Driven Science and Its Consequences
summary: Artificial intelligence is transforming scientific research - not merely as a more powerful instrument, but as an autonomous participant in the research cycle itself. This transition constitutes, in the most precise sense of the term, the industrialization of research: a shift from a craft model, in which knowledge, method, and judgment are embedded in the researcher, to a pipeline model, in which these steps are decomposed, automated, and supervised. The US Department of Energy's Genesis Mission is the most ambitious current instantiation of this shift, but the fundamental questions it raises extend far beyond any single program. This essay examines seven such questions: the erosion of the intergenerational transmission of scientific competence; the growing opacity of AI-generated theories; the collapse of peer evaluation under a flood of machine-generated output; the unproven capacity of AI for paradigm-shifting discovery; the capture of the scientific agenda by political and industrial actors; the compounding of systematic errors in closed-loop pipelines; and the structural bifurcation of the global research community into incommensurable tiers. These concerns do not constitute an argument against AI-driven science - whose demonstrated potential is real and significant. They constitute the conditions under which that potential can be responsibly pursued.

AI.tldr: The paper examines the transformative impact of AI on scientific research, framing it as an 'industrialization of research' shift from craft-based to pipeline models, and raises seven critical questions about its implications for scientific practice and community.
AI.motivation: AI is fundamentally changing scientific research by becoming an autonomous participant rather than just a tool, representing a shift from craft-based to industrialized pipeline models. This transformation, exemplified by initiatives like the DOE's Genesis Mission, raises profound questions about the future of scientific inquiry that extend beyond any single program.
AI.method: The paper uses an analytical essay approach to examine seven critical questions arising from AI's role in scientific research. It employs a conceptual framework viewing AI-driven research as 'industrialization' and analyzes its implications across scientific, social, and structural dimensions.
AI.result: The paper identifies seven key concerns: erosion of scientific competence transmission, opacity of AI-generated theories, collapse of peer review under machine output, unproven AI capacity for paradigm shifts, political/industrial agenda capture, compounding systematic errors in closed-loop systems, and global research community bifurcation into unequal tiers.
AI.conclusion: While acknowledging the real potential of AI-driven science, the paper argues that these seven concerns don't constitute opposition to AI but rather define the essential conditions under which this potential must be responsibly pursued to ensure the integrity and sustainability of scientific research.

title: Learning in Infinitesimal Non-Compositional Sketches
summary: This paper develops a categorical framework -- Learning in Infinitesimal Non-Compositional Sketches (LINCS) -- as the repair of non-compositionality: failures of diagrams to factor through quotient sketches lifted to the tangent category setting. Machine learning problems are specified as sketches: graphs with commutativity conditions $\mathcal D$, limit cones $\mathcal L$, and colimit cocones $\mathcal K$, generalizing the usual scalarization of loss functions or vector space assumptions. Non-compositionality is defined purely as failure of a universal factorization problem, not as arithmetic error between the desired and actual predictions. Given a learning sketch $\mathbb S=(S,\mathcal D,\mathcal L,\mathcal K)$, whose underlying graph is $S$, and a model $D:J \rightarrow C$, the base defect is the obstruction to factorization $\mbox{Obs}(\mbox{Fact}_{\mathbb S}(D))$. The tangent lift applies the tangent functor $T$ to obtain $TD:J \rightarrow C$, and LINCS is defined as the obstruction $\mbox{Obs}(\mbox{Fact}_{\mathbb S}(TD))$ -- asking whether infinitesimal perturbations preserve the compositionality constraints.The paper also introduces Tangent Learning Sketches, which are sketches equipped with Cockett-Cruttwell tangent structure. The paper defines the INC endofunctor, which iterates the tangent lift, producing a tower $D,TD,T^2D, \cdots$ of factorization problems. ML is thereby formulated as the search for a coalgebraic fixed point where successive tangent unfoldings stabilize ($νT_{\mbox{INC}}$). Using the Aczel--Mendler theorem, we prove existence of a final INC coalgebra whenever $T_{\mbox{INC}}$ admits a set-based class realization that creates its final carrier. A detailed experimental evaluation of LINCS is underway in a number of concrete ML settings, including deep learning, large language models, and reinforcement learning, and is described in companion papers.

AI.tldr: LINCS provides a categorical framework using sketches and tangent categories to address non-compositionality in machine learning by repairing factorization failures through infinitesimal perturbations.
AI.motivation: To address failures of compositionality in machine learning models, where traditional approaches treat errors as arithmetic discrepancies rather than structural factorization failures.
AI.method: Develops a categorical framework using learning sketches (graphs with commutativity conditions, limit cones, colimit cocones) and tangent functors to lift factorization problems, then defines the INC endofunctor for iterative tangent unfolding.
AI.result: Formalizes machine learning as a coalgebraic fixed point problem (νT_inc) and proves existence of final INC coalgebras using the Aczel-Mendler theorem, enabling repair of non-compositionality.
AI.conclusion: LINCS offers a principled categorical foundation for machine learning that reconceptualizes learning as repairing factorization failures through tangent perturbations rather than minimizing scalar losses.

title: MedFailBench: A Clinician-Built Open-Source Benchmark for Medical AI Safety Boundary Inspection
comment: 6 pages; clinician-reviewed synthetic benchmark; no patient data
summary: Most medical AI benchmarks measure whether a model knows the correct answer. MedFailBench asks a different question: which safety boundary failed? We present a clinician-built synthetic benchmark and failure atlas that labels medical AI errors by severity (1--5) and safety gate type (missed urgent escalation, unsafe remote dosing, unsafe discharge reassurance, evidence fabrication, unsafe protocol execution, source support gap). The current public release (v0.2.1) contains 44 clinician-reviewed synthetic cases with severity annotations, a live HuggingFace leaderboard preview, a safety gate taxonomy, a clinical severity rubric, and an automated pipeline for archiving model-response screening runs. No patient data, clinical validation claims, or model rankings are included. MedFailBench is released under Apache-2.0 and CC-BY-4.0 and carries the Zenodo DOI 10.5281/zenodo.21205535.

AI.tldr: MedFailBench introduces a synthetic medical AI benchmark focused on identifying which safety boundaries fail, categorizing errors by severity (1-5) and safety gate types, rather than just measuring correct answers.
AI.motivation: Most medical AI benchmarks only measure whether models provide correct answers, but don't identify specific safety boundary failures in clinical contexts. There's a need to systematically categorize and understand medical AI errors by severity and failure types to improve safety assessments.
AI.method: Created a clinician-built synthetic benchmark with 44 clinician-reviewed synthetic cases, including severity annotations, safety gate taxonomy, clinical severity rubric, automated pipeline for archiving model-response screening runs, and a live HuggingFace leaderboard preview.
AI.result: Developed MedFailBench v0.2.1 with a comprehensive failure atlas labeling errors by severity (1-5) and safety gate types: missed urgent escalation, unsafe remote dosing, unsafe discharge reassurance, evidence fabrication, unsafe protocol execution, and source support gap.
AI.conclusion: MedFailBench provides a new approach to evaluating medical AI safety by focusing on failure types and severity rather than just correctness, offering tools for systematic safety boundary assessment while avoiding patient data use and clinical validation claims.

title: On-Policy Delta Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation is an alternative post-training method in reinforcement learning that alleviates the constraints imposed by reward models by providing token-level supervision from a teacher model. Although on-policy distillation has been studied and applied across various settings, its fundamental design remains underexplored. In this paper, we introduce a new distillation reward, termed the delta signal, instead of directly imitating the teacher's output distribution. The delta signal is defined as the difference between the teacher model and its base model prior to instruction tuning for reasoning capability. It therefore captures the changes induced by reasoning tuning and provides a more direct signal for transferring reasoning capabilities. Using extensive empirical evidence, we show that the delta signal substantially improves on-policy distillation and refer to the new distillation method as On-Policy Delta Distillation (OPD$^2$). Experiments across mathematics, science, and code-reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that OPD$^2$ consistently outperforms conventional on-policy distillation, enabling reasoning LLMs to achieve strong performance with only a short post-training period. Code will be available at https://github.com/naver-ai/opd2

AI.tldr: OPD² (On-Policy Delta Distillation) introduces a new delta signal reward for distillation that captures reasoning capability changes between teacher and base models, substantially improving on-policy distillation for LLMs across multiple reasoning domains.
AI.motivation: Current on-policy distillation methods in RL rely on token-level supervision but have underexplored fundamental design. Direct imitation of teacher output distributions may not effectively transfer reasoning capabilities. There's a need for better distillation signals that specifically capture reasoning improvements from instruction tuning.
AI.method: Proposes OPD² (On-Policy Delta Distillation) which uses a delta signal instead of direct imitation. The delta signal is defined as the difference between the teacher model and its base model prior to instruction tuning, capturing reasoning capability changes induced by tuning. This provides more direct signal for transferring reasoning abilities.
AI.result: Extensive empirical evidence shows delta signal substantially improves on-policy distillation. OPD² consistently outperforms conventional on-policy distillation across mathematics, science, and code-reasoning benchmarks. Enables reasoning LLMs to achieve strong performance with only short post-training periods.
AI.conclusion: OPD² represents a significant improvement in on-policy distillation methodology by using delta signals that better capture reasoning capability transfers. The approach effectively enables reasoning LLMs to achieve strong performance quickly through focused post-training, advancing the state of knowledge distillation in reinforcement learning for language models.

title: Benchmarking Multimodal Large Language Models for Scientific Visualization Literacy
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly used to interpret visualizations, yet current evaluations remain largely chart-centric and provide limited evidence of understanding of scientific visualization (SciVis). We benchmark six MLLMs on the scientific visualization literacy assessment test, a standardized SciVis literacy assessment comprising 49 items based on 18 scientific visualizations and illustrations, spanning 8 techniques and 11 task types. We evaluate three closed-source and three open-source models under a closed-world protocol and compare their performance using data from 485 human participants. Results show that current MLLMs do not exhibit uniform SciVis literacy. Gemini is the strongest model overall, exceeding the human mean across the evaluated subsets, whereas the open-source models remain below the human baseline. Performance is highly uneven across techniques and tasks: models perform best on scientific illustration, search, and spatial understanding, but struggle on texture-based and integration-based visualizations and on quantitative estimation. Error analysis reveals recurring failures in fine-grained quantitative estimation, flow-direction interpretation, and grounded encoding interpretation. These findings position SciVis literacy as a necessary benchmark dimension for evaluating multimodal AI systems. Our code and model outputs are publicly available at https://github.com/patdmp/mllm-scivis-lit-benchmark.

AI.tldr: MLLMs struggle with scientific visualization literacy, performing unevenly across techniques and tasks, with closed-source models generally outperforming open-source ones but still showing significant gaps in quantitative estimation and specialized SciVis tasks.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal LLM evaluations are too chart-centric and lack proper assessment of scientific visualization (SciVis) understanding, which is critical for evaluating multimodal AI systems in scientific contexts.
AI.method: Benchmarked six MLLMs (three closed-source, three open-source) on a standardized SciVis literacy assessment test comprising 49 items based on 18 scientific visualizations, covering 8 techniques and 11 task types, comparing against 485 human participants under closed-world protocol.
AI.result: Current MLLMs don't show uniform SciVis literacy; Gemini is strongest overall (exceeding human mean), open-source models remain below human baseline. Performance varies significantly: models excel at scientific illustration, search, and spatial tasks but struggle with texture/integration visualizations and quantitative estimation. Common failures include fine-grained quantitative estimation, flow-direction interpretation, and grounded encoding interpretation.
AI.conclusion: SciVis literacy should be a necessary benchmark dimension for evaluating multimodal AI systems, as current MLLMs have significant gaps in specialized scientific visualization understanding despite some strong performance in basic visualization tasks.

title: RTS Smoother-Guided Learning of Physics-Based Neural Differential Models
summary: Ordinary differential equations (ODEs) are widely used to model dynamical systems in physics, biology, neuroscience, and physiology, but in many applications some equations of the dynamics are unknown and only a subset of the state variables are measured. We propose a hybrid neural--physics framework in which the known components of the ODE are kept explicit and the missing components are represented by a neural network. The proposed method consists of two stages where we alternate between state and parameter estimation and iterate until a predetermined criterion is met. Specifically, in the first step, we treat the model parameters as being known and we infer the latent states from the available measurements using a Rauch--Tung--Striebel (RTS) smoother. In the second stage, we treat the smoothed trajectories as being known and use them to estimate the neural networks' parameters through backpropagation. We evaluate the method on benchmark systems spanning linear, nonlinear, and stiff dynamics under partial state observation. Across these settings, the proposed method learns missing ODE components from incomplete measurements while exploiting and retaining interpretable mechanistic structure and improving latent-state reconstruction and long-horizon prediction.

AI.tldr: A hybrid neural-physics framework for learning unknown ODE components from partial observations by alternating between state estimation with RTS smoother and neural network parameter learning.
AI.motivation: Many real-world dynamical systems modeled by ODEs have unknown equations and only partial state measurements are available, creating a need for methods that can learn missing components while preserving known physics.
AI.method: Two-stage alternating approach: (1) State estimation using Rauch-Tung-Striebel smoother with known parameters, (2) Neural network parameter estimation through backpropagation using smoothed trajectories. The method maintains known ODE components explicitly while modeling unknown parts with neural networks.
AI.result: Successfully tested on linear, nonlinear, and stiff benchmark systems with partial state observations. The method learns missing ODE components while preserving interpretable mechanistic structure, improving both latent-state reconstruction and long-horizon prediction.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid neural-physics framework effectively combines interpretable physics-based modeling with neural network flexibility to handle incomplete dynamical systems, providing accurate reconstruction and prediction while maintaining model interpretability where possible.

title: Can We Trust Item Response Theory for AI Evaluation?
summary: AI benchmarks increasingly leverage item-level statistical models, particularly item response theory (IRT), to estimate model capabilities, rank systems, select informative examples, and diagnose benchmark quality. However, AI benchmark data often departs from the data regime of human testing, for which standard IRT estimation tools were originally developed: benchmarks typically involve fewer evaluated models, far more items, and capability distributions that may be skewed, clustered, or multimodal. We examine how these regime mismatches challenge the reliability of IRT modeling for AI evaluation. Using item parameters and capability distributions derived from six widely used LLM benchmarks, we simulate response matrices under three common IRT models and compare four estimation tools used in recent benchmark studies: marginal maximum likelihood, Markov chain Monte Carlo, variational inference, and a neural pseudo-Siamese estimator. Across 18,000 simulation conditions, we systematically evaluate computational feasibility, scalability, and the reliability of IRT inferences about model rankings, predicted performance, and item characteristics. Results show that classical estimators can become infeasible in large benchmark settings, whereas scalable estimators can produce unreliable item-level and ranking inferences with small or nonnormally distributed model sets. This study identifies when latent trait models reliably support or risk distorting AI benchmarking claims, and what sample sizes and diagnostics are needed for trustworthy use.

AI.tldr: IRT models for AI benchmark evaluation face reliability challenges due to data regime mismatches with human testing, and researchers need to carefully consider estimation methods and sample sizes for trustworthy results.
AI.motivation: AI benchmarks increasingly use IRT models to evaluate model capabilities, but these models were originally developed for human testing data regimes. AI benchmark data differs significantly with fewer models, more items, and non-normal capability distributions, raising questions about IRT reliability for AI evaluation.
AI.method: Used item parameters and capability distributions from six widely used LLM benchmarks to simulate response matrices under three common IRT models. Systematically evaluated four estimation tools (marginal maximum likelihood, MCMC, variational inference, neural pseudo-Siamese estimator) across 18,000 simulation conditions. Assessed computational feasibility, scalability, and reliability of IRT inferences.
AI.result: Classical IRT estimators become infeasible in large benchmark settings, while scalable estimators can produce unreliable item-level and ranking inferences with small or non-normally distributed model sets. Reliability varies significantly across different estimation methods and data conditions.
AI.conclusion: The study identifies when latent trait models can reliably support or risk distorting AI benchmarking claims, and provides guidance on necessary sample sizes and diagnostics for trustworthy IRT usage in AI evaluation contexts.

title: BadWAM: When World-Action Models Dream Right but Act Wrong
summary: World-action models (WAMs) are emerging as a promising foundation for embodied control: rather than predicting actions alone, they learn representations that couple action generation with future world prediction.
