This coupling is often viewed as a source of robustness, interpretability, and safety, as a robot's action can in principle be checked against its imagined future. In this paper, we show that this assumption is fragile. We introduce BadWAM, a unified framework for modeling and evaluating World-Action Drift Attacks: a new class of WAM-specific adversarial attacks that use small visual perturbations to break the alignment between what a WAM imagines and what it executes. BadWAM characterizes this attack surface along two natural criteria: attack strength and stealthiness. When the adversary prioritizes disruption, BadWAM instantiates an action-only adversarial attack, which directly drives the model toward task-failing actions. When the adversary additionally prioritizes stealth, BadWAM instantiates an imagination-preserving adversarial attack, which seeks to induce harmful action shifts while keeping the model's predicted future close to its clean imagination. Together, these two attacks capture a spectrum of WAM-specific failures: from overt action hijacking to stealthier cases where the model appears to imagine a plausible future but executes a desynchronized action. We evaluate BadWAM across different variants of WAMs. Results show that our attacks substantially reduce task success rates under closed-loop execution. For example, our action-only attack reduces the model performance from 96.5% to 43.1% success. The results of our imagination-preserving attack further exposes a WAM-specific vulnerability: moderate future-preserving regularization can maintain strong attack performance while reducing future imagination drift.

AI.tldr: BadWAM is a framework that demonstrates how world-action models (WAMs) are vulnerable to adversarial attacks that break the alignment between predicted futures and executed actions, enabling both overt and stealthy attacks that significantly reduce task performance.
AI.motivation: While world-action models (WAMs) are viewed as promising for embodied control due to their coupling of action generation with future world prediction—which provides robustness, interpretability, and safety—this paper challenges that assumption by showing this coupling is fragile and can be exploited by adversaries.
AI.method: The authors introduce BadWAM, a unified framework for modeling and evaluating World-Action Drift Attacks. They characterize attacks along two dimensions: attack strength and stealthiness. They instantiate two main attack types: 1) action-only adversarial attacks that directly drive models toward task-failing actions, and 2) imagination-preserving adversarial attacks that induce harmful action shifts while keeping predicted futures close to clean imagination. These are evaluated across different WAM variants.
AI.result: The attacks substantially reduce task success rates under closed-loop execution. The action-only attack reduces model performance from 96.5% to 43.1% success. The imagination-preserving attack reveals a WAM-specific vulnerability: moderate future-preserving regularization can maintain strong attack performance while reducing future imagination drift, allowing for stealthier attacks.
AI.conclusion: World-action models have inherent vulnerabilities despite their promised benefits. The coupling between action generation and future prediction, often seen as a safety feature, can be exploited through carefully crafted adversarial attacks that break this alignment. This exposes a critical security flaw in WAM-based systems that needs addressing for safe real-world deployment.

title: Plover: Steering GUI Agents through Plan-Centric Interaction
summary: Graphical user interface (GUI) automation remains challenging in real-world environments, where dynamic layouts, unexpected dialogs, and evolving interface states can cause autonomous agents to drift from user intent. Recent vision-based multimodal agents improve flexibility by operating directly over screenshots and natural language instructions, but planning and adaptation often remain internal, limiting users' ability to inspect, supervise, or correct system behavior. We present Plover, a plan-centric vision-based GUI automation system that externalizes task plans and replanning as persistent, inspectable, and revisable artifacts. Through a planner--executor architecture, Plover supports explicit supervision of evolving execution, localized correction through editable plans, natural-language guidance, and screenshot-grounded interventions, while preserving prior progress during repair. A formative study with six participants informed the interaction design. We then evaluate Plover through benchmark failure-case repair and scenario-based workflow analyses. Our results show that many autonomous GUI-agent failures are structurally repairable when plans remain visible and interventions are localized, and that explicit replanning helps make GUI automation more transparent, controllable, and adaptable.

AI.tldr: Plover is a vision-based GUI automation system that externalizes task plans and replanning as persistent, inspectable artifacts, enabling user supervision, correction, and natural-language interventions while preserving progress during repairs.
AI.motivation: GUI automation is challenging in real-world environments with dynamic layouts and unexpected dialogs. While vision-based multimodal agents improve flexibility, they often keep planning internal, limiting users' ability to inspect, supervise, or correct system behavior.
AI.method: Plover uses a planner-executor architecture that externalizes task plans and replanning as persistent, inspectable artifacts. It supports explicit supervision of execution, localized correction through editable plans, natural-language guidance, and screenshot-grounded interventions while preserving prior progress during repair.
AI.result: Evaluation shows that many autonomous GUI-agent failures are structurally repairable when plans remain visible and interventions are localized. Formative study with six participants informed interaction design, and benchmark failure-case repair and scenario-based workflow analyses demonstrate effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Explicit replanning and externalizing plans help make GUI automation more transparent, controllable, and adaptable by enabling user supervision and correction while preserving progress during repairs.

title: Data Driven Block Replacement Scheduling
summary: We develop data-driven algorithms for maintaining $N$ independent identical machines under a \textit{block replacement policy}, in which each machine is replaced upon failure and all machines are jointly replaced at regular intervals of length $k$. The goal is to learn the cost-minimizing interval $k^*$ from operational data when the lifetime distribution is unknown. At each decision epoch, the operator selects $k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$, observes the resulting failure history (a mixture of complete and right-censored lifetimes) and incurs a per-unit-time cost governed by the renewal function. We formulate this as a stochastic multi-armed bandit and propose Hoeffding- and Bernstein-based lower-confidence-bound algorithms achieving $O(K \log T)$ regret, matching the Lai--Robbins lower bound. Exploiting a nested observation property unique to block replacement, correlated variants attain $O((K-k^*)\log T)$ regret and require only $O(1)$ direct pulls of suboptimal arms $k < k^*$. A complementary Kaplan--Meier renewal algorithm estimates the lifetime distribution nonparametrically from censored data, achieving almost-sure policy consistency and empirically near-zero incremental regret at long horizons. We additionally analyze two average-cost MDPs: a time-elapsed formulation establishing that block replacement is optimal within its policy class for any lifetime distribution, and an age-vector formulation proving a monotone threshold structure under increasing failure rate distributions and providing a gold-standard cost benchmark. Numerical experiments confirm the theoretical ordering and reveal structural cost gaps between optimal block and age-dependent replacement.

AI.tldr: Develop data-driven algorithms for optimal block replacement policy in maintenance of N identical machines with unknown lifetime distribution, achieving logarithmic regret through bandit algorithms and proving theoretical properties.
AI.motivation: Need to learn cost-minimizing replacement interval k* from operational data when machine lifetime distribution is unknown, addressing maintenance optimization for identical machines under block replacement policies.
AI.method: Formulate as stochastic multi-armed bandit problem; propose Hoeffding- and Bernstein-based LCB algorithms; exploit nested observation property for correlated variants; develop Kaplan-Meier renewal algorithm for nonparametric estimation; analyze two average-cost MDPs (time-elapsed and age-vector formulations).
AI.result: Achieve O(K log T) regret matching Lai-Robbins lower bound; correlated variants attain O((K-k*)log T) regret with O(1) direct pulls of suboptimal arms; prove block replacement optimal within policy class; show monotone threshold structure under IFR distributions; demonstrate near-zero incremental regret empirically.
AI.conclusion: Data-driven algorithms effectively learn optimal block replacement intervals with strong theoretical guarantees and practical performance, providing theoretical ordering and revealing cost gaps between optimal block and age-dependent replacement policies.

title: Mutable Low-Rank Sketches for Retrain-Free Recommendation
summary: A common bottleneck in two-stage recommendation is embedding staleness: when a user rates a new item, their embedding remains fixed until the next retrain cycle. We propose mutable sketches, which store each user's preferences in a KP-tree (a sparse segment tree with sum aggregation), fit a low-rank projection once, and recompute embeddings on-the-fly as ratings arrive. We prove that each new observation monotonically tightens the prediction error envelope (Theorem 1), a guarantee that FunkSVD and eALS lack. On KuaiRec, the mutable sketch achieves 0.810 RMSE at 1.8% data read vs. ALS 0.822 at 100%, with 8x faster per-batch updates. A new user receives personalized recommendations in <1 ms after their first rating, with no model retraining required. A comparison of sampling strategies across density regimes shows that the KP-tree's norm-proportional sampling provides 40-130% better item coverage on sparse data (<1% density), while uniform sampling suffices on dense matrices.

AI.tldr: Mutable sketches enable real-time user embedding updates in two-stage recommendation systems using KP-trees and low-rank projections, eliminating embedding staleness without model retraining.
AI.motivation: Address embedding staleness in two-stage recommendation systems where user embeddings remain fixed after new ratings until the next retrain cycle, causing outdated recommendations.
AI.method: Proposes mutable sketches that store user preferences in KP-trees (sparse segment trees with sum aggregation), fit a low-rank projection once, and recompute embeddings on-the-fly as new ratings arrive.
AI.result: Achieves 0.810 RMSE at 1.8% data read vs ALS 0.822 at 100%, with 8x faster per-batch updates; new users get personalized recommendations in <1 ms after first rating; KP-tree's norm-proportional sampling provides 40-130% better item coverage on sparse data.
AI.conclusion: Mutable sketches successfully solve embedding staleness in two-stage recommendation systems, offering provable error guarantees, efficient real-time updates, and adaptive sampling strategies for different data density regimes.

title: When Words Are Safe But Actions Kill: Probing Physical Danger Beyond Text Safety in Hidden-State Risk Space
summary: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly serve as high-level planners for embodied agents, where linguistically benign instructions can become unsafe once grounded in the physical world. We study whether this physically grounded danger is the same safety problem as ordinary text-level content danger. Through hidden-state direction analysis and random-split null tests, we show that content danger (CD) and physical danger (PD) form separable signals in LLM representations across Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B/32B, Phi-3.5 and SmolLM2. Building on the CD/PD separability, we propose PRISM, a single-layer L2-regularized logistic probe over full hidden states. PRISM achieves 86.2--87.7\% accuracy on SafeAgentBench with 11.7--13.7\% FPR, while same-scale LLM judges over-block safe tasks at 24.7--39.0\% FPR. We further introduce PhysicalSafetyBench-1K (PSB-1K), a contrastive benchmark of 1{,}000 physical-risk pairs without direct harm keywords, to test whether methods detect physically grounded danger rather than explicit unsafe wording. On PSB-1K, PRISM reaches 99.6\% accuracy and 0.7\% FPR, whereas a Qwen2.5-3B judge rejects 67.8\% of safe tasks. PRISM also replicates on SafeText and EARBench, supporting hidden-state probing as a representation-level method for physical safety beyond text moderation.

AI.tldr: PRISM is a single-layer logistic probe that detects physically grounded danger in LLM representations with high accuracy (86-87% on SafeAgentBench, 99.6% on PSB-1K) by separating content danger from physical danger signals.
AI.motivation: When LLMs serve as planners for embodied agents, linguistically benign instructions can become unsafe when grounded in the physical world. The paper questions whether physically grounded danger is the same safety problem as ordinary text-level content danger.
AI.method: 1. Shows content danger (CD) and physical danger (PD) are separable signals in LLM representations via hidden-state direction analysis and random-split null tests. 2. Introduces PRISM: a single-layer L2-regularized logistic probe over full hidden states. 3. Creates PhysicalSafetyBench-1K (PSB-1K), a contrastive benchmark of 1,000 physical-risk pairs without direct harm keywords.
AI.result: PRISM achieves 86.2-87.7% accuracy on SafeAgentBench with 11.7-13.7% FPR, while same-scale LLM judges over-block safe tasks at 24.7-39.0% FPR. On PSB-1K, PRISM reaches 99.6% accuracy and 0.7% FPR, outperforming Qwen2.5-3B judge (67.8% safe tasks rejected). PRISM also generalizes to SafeText and EARBench.
AI.conclusion: Content danger and physical danger are separable phenomena in LLM representations. Hidden-state probing (PRISM) provides an effective representation-level method for detecting physically grounded danger beyond text moderation, addressing a distinct safety challenge for embodied AI systems.

title: Decoding Market Emotion from Blockchain Activity: A Data-Driven Sentiment Classifier
comment: This manuscript has been accepted for presentation at the IEEE International Symposium on Computers and Communications (ISCC 2026)
summary: The growing use of Bitcoin as a decentralized digital asset and investment tool has sparked strong interest in understanding its market behavior. This study presents a new approach to analyze Bitcoin market sentiment by combining on-chain and financial data with social media posts. Unlike models that aim to predict prices, this work focuses on explaining market sentiment using blockchain transactions, historical price data of Bitcoin, and daily Twitter sentiment classifications. The method merges sentiment trends with on-chain and financial metrics, normalized into a dataset for detailed market analysis. Multiple machine learning models were tested using cross-validation, with Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) emerging as the most reliable model for classifying sentiment, achieving an average F1-score of about 0.84. SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), a game theory-based method for model interpretability, was used to quantify the contribution of on-chain features to the model's predictions, improving transparency. The results indicate that this data combination yields meaningful predictive signals and insights, supporting data-driven cryptocurrency analysis and future improvements with deep learning.

AI.tldr: A novel Bitcoin market sentiment analysis approach combining on-chain data, financial metrics, and Twitter sentiment using Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) achieves 0.84 F1-score, with SHAP providing interpretability.
AI.motivation: Bitcoin's growing importance as a decentralized digital asset and investment tool creates need for better understanding of market behavior, particularly sentiment analysis beyond just price prediction.
AI.method: Combines on-chain data (blockchain transactions), financial data (historical Bitcoin prices), and social media data (daily Twitter sentiment classifications). Normalizes into unified dataset, tests multiple ML models with cross-validation, uses XGBoost for classification, and applies SHAP for feature importance analysis.
AI.result: Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) emerged as most reliable model with average F1-score of ~0.84 for sentiment classification. SHAP analysis quantified contribution of on-chain features, improving model transparency and interpretability.
AI.conclusion: Combining on-chain, financial, and social media data provides meaningful predictive signals for Bitcoin market sentiment, supporting data-driven cryptocurrency analysis and promising future improvements with deep learning approaches.

title: AutoSynthesis: An agentic system for automated meta-analysis
summary: Evidence synthesis is crucial for turning primary research into reliable knowledge for science, medicine, education, and policy. Yet, quantitative evidence synthesis remains largely manual and difficult to scale. Here, we introduce AutoSynthesis, an end-to-end multi-agent system for automated meta-analysis. Given a research question in natural language, AutoSynthesis formulates a search strategy, retrieves scientific literature, screens candidate studies, assesses full-text eligibility, extracts quantitative statistics, computes standardized effect sizes, and finally performs random-effects meta-analysis. AutoSynthesis further supports heterogeneity analysis to examine how effect sizes vary across moderators, as well as risk-of-bias assessment. As output, AutoSynthesis produces a transparent report aligned with PRISMA guidelines. In our application, AutoSynthesis screened over 28 studies and extracted more than 20 quantitative claims. The pooled effect estimates produced by AutoSynthesis are similar to Hedges' $g$ of expert-conducted meta-analyses, indicating close agreement with manual evidence synthesis. Together, these results show that AutoSynthesis can make quantitative evidence synthesis more scalable, thereby supporting evidence-based decision-making across disciplines.

AI.tldr: AutoSynthesis is an end-to-end multi-agent system that automates meta-analysis from research questions in natural language through search, screening, extraction, and statistical analysis, producing PRISMA-aligned reports with results comparable to manual expert synthesis.
AI.motivation: Quantitative evidence synthesis is crucial for reliable knowledge generation across science, medicine, education, and policy, but current methods are largely manual and difficult to scale, creating bottlenecks in evidence-based decision-making.
AI.method: AutoSynthesis is a multi-agent system that takes natural language research questions and performs: search strategy formulation, literature retrieval, study screening, full-text eligibility assessment, quantitative statistics extraction, standardized effect size computation, and random-effects meta-analysis with heterogeneity analysis and risk-of-bias assessment.
AI.result: The system screened over 28 studies and extracted more than 20 quantitative claims. Pooled effect estimates (Hedges' g) produced by AutoSynthesis showed close agreement with expert-conducted meta-analyses, demonstrating comparable accuracy to manual evidence synthesis.
AI.conclusion: AutoSynthesis can make quantitative evidence synthesis more scalable while maintaining quality comparable to expert manual methods, thereby supporting evidence-based decision-making across multiple disciplines through automated, transparent meta-analysis.

title: teLLMe Why (Ain't Nothing but a Jam): Exploratory Causal Analysis of Urban Driving Data
comment: Accepted at the NeurIPS 2025 Workshop on UrbanAI. 6 pages
summary: Traffic agencies now have access to large volumes of video-derived data for studying safety and congestion. Most of these data are observational and collected without interventions, which makes causal questions such as "How would rain change traffic density?" difficult to answer. We present teLLMe, a system for exploratory causal analysis of urban driving datasets. The system starts from a structured event table built from dashcam annotations and combines causal structure learning with the PC algorithm, bootstrap-based stability checks, and query-specific effect estimation using linear regression and DoWhy. Natural-language questions are mapped to structured causal queries through a schema-aware LLM, enabling users to specify treatments, outcomes, and subpopulations. teLLMe returns a "Causal Card" that summarizes effect estimates, adjustment sets, DAG support, and assumptions, followed by a short natural-language explanation. Case studies on BDD-derived traffic events show that the system can surface plausible relationships involving weather, peak hours, and traffic density, while making uncertainty and modeling choices explicit. The system is designed as a tool for hypothesis generation and expert reasoning rather than a source of definitive causal claims.

AI.tldr: teLLMe is a system for causal analysis of urban driving data that combines causal structure learning with natural language queries to generate "Causal Cards" summarizing effects and assumptions.
AI.motivation: Traffic agencies have large volumes of observational video data, but causal questions (like how rain affects traffic density) are difficult to answer with observational data alone since there are no interventions.
AI.method: System combines causal structure learning (PC algorithm), bootstrap-based stability checks, and query-specific effect estimation using linear regression and DoWhy. Natural-language questions are mapped to structured causal queries via schema-aware LLM.
AI.result: Case studies on BDD-derived traffic events show system can surface plausible relationships involving weather, peak hours, and traffic density while making uncertainty and modeling choices explicit.
AI.conclusion: System is designed as a tool for hypothesis generation and expert reasoning rather than a source of definitive causal claims, with emphasis on transparency through Causal Cards that summarize effect estimates, adjustment sets, DAG support, and assumptions.

title: SearchOS-V1: Towards Robust Open-Domain Information-Seeking Agent Collaboration
summary: Recent advances in Tool-Integrated Large Language Models have made web search a core capability of information-seeking agents. However, as interaction histories grow, agents increasingly struggle to track task progress. When search attempts fail to yield useful evidence, current single- and multi-agent systems can become trapped in repetitive loops, wasting search budgets and ultimately compromising the quality and completeness of the final output. We introduce SearchOS, a system-level multi-agent framework that turns fragile, implicit search progress into explicit, persistent, and shared state. First, we formulate open-domain information seeking as relational schema completion with grounded citations, where agents discover entities, populate attributes across linked tables, and anchor each value to source evidence. Then we design Search-Oriented Context Management (SOCM), which externalizes the evolving state into Frontier Task, an Evidence Graph, a Coverage Map, and Failure Memory. Built on SOCM, SearchOS applies a pipeline-parallel scheduling mechanism that overlaps the execution of sub-agents and continuously refills freed slots with tasks targeting unresolved coverage gaps to improve utilization and throughput. To schedule and control the execution of search agents, SearchOS introduces a Search Tool Middleware Harness that intercepts model and tool interactions to record grounded evidence and react to stalls or budget exhaustion, and provides a reusable hierarchical skill system comprising strategy and access skills to augment the agents' search process and avoid repeating failed search patterns across runs. On WideSearch and GISA, SearchOS leads all metrics among the evaluated single- and multi-agent baselines, paving the way toward robust information-seeking collaboration.

AI.tldr: SearchOS is a multi-agent framework that improves information-seeking agents by tracking search progress explicitly, preventing repetitive search loops, and optimizing search execution through shared state management.
AI.motivation: Current single- and multi-agent systems for web search struggle with tracking task progress as interaction histories grow. When search attempts fail, agents can become trapped in repetitive loops, wasting search budgets and compromising final output quality. There's a need to turn implicit search progress into explicit, persistent state.
AI.method:
1. Formulate open-domain information seeking as relational schema completion with grounded citations
2. Design Search-Oriented Context Management (SOCM) that externalizes state into Frontier Task, Evidence Graph, Coverage Map, and Failure Memory
3. Implement pipeline-parallel scheduling that overlaps sub-agent execution and refills tasks targeting unresolved coverage gaps
4. Introduce Search Tool Middleware Harness to intercept model/tool interactions, record evidence, and react to stalls
5. Provide reusable hierarchical skill system with strategy and access skills to avoid repeating failed search patterns
AI.result: SearchOS leads all metrics among evaluated single- and multi-agent baselines on WideSearch and GISA datasets, demonstrating improved information-seeking collaboration.
AI.conclusion: SearchOS successfully transforms fragile, implicit search progress into explicit, persistent state, enabling more robust information-seeking collaboration and paving the way for improved multi-agent search systems.

title: Pretraining Data Can Be Poisoned through Computational Propaganda
summary: Poisoning pretraining data can introduce harmful behaviors to LMs that are difficult to detect and mitigate. Prior work on poisoning pretraining data has largely exploited established data sources such as Wikipedia, which do not represent the large scale and heterogeneity typical of pretraining corpora, and has ignored the interaction between poisoned data and data curation pipelines. We demonstrate that poisoning attacks on pretraining data are feasible beyond this limited setting through an existing web-scale content injection mechanism: public discussion interfaces. Additionally, to measure whether malicious content is included after web crawling and data curation, we introduce HalfLife, a novel analysis for estimating adversarial content inclusion in web-crawl based LM training data. We use HalfLife to explore the feasibility of poisoning pretraining corpora at web scale through open discussion interfaces. Our analysis demonstrates the importance of estimating whether poison injections are included in pretraining data, and establishes third-party webpage content as a possible vector for attacking language model pretraining.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that poisoning attacks on LM pretraining data are feasible via public discussion interfaces and introduces HalfLife, a novel method to estimate adversarial content inclusion in web-crawl training data.
AI.motivation: Prior work on poisoning pretraining data has been limited to established data sources like Wikipedia, which don't represent the scale and heterogeneity of real pretraining corpora. Also, previous research has ignored the interaction between poisoned data and data curation pipelines.
AI.method: The paper uses an existing web-scale content injection mechanism (public discussion interfaces) for poisoning attacks. It introduces HalfLife, a novel analysis method for estimating adversarial content inclusion in web-crawl based LM training data, specifically measuring whether malicious content survives web crawling and data curation processes.
AI.result: The analysis demonstrates that poisoning pretraining corpora at web scale through open discussion interfaces is feasible. Third-party webpage content serves as a possible vector for attacking language model pretraining, and the study shows the importance of estimating whether poison injections actually get included in pretraining data.
AI.conclusion: Poisoning attacks on pretraining data are more feasible than previously thought when considering real-world web-scale content injection mechanisms. The HalfLife analysis method provides a crucial tool for measuring adversarial content inclusion, highlighting the need to account for data curation pipelines in security assessments of language models.
